data.title: China approves Pfizer’s GLP-1 drug for weight management, intensifying market competition
data.description:
• China has approved a new obesity drug from Pfizer (PFE), intensifying competition in a market that is expected to grow even more crowded with upcoming generic versions.
  • The drug, ecnoglutide, is approved for chronic weight management in overweight or obese adults, Pfizer (PFE) announced on Friday via WeChat, according to reports. The company secured China rights to the therapy from local startup Hangzhou Sciwind Bioscience in a $495 million deal in late February. Ecnoglutide ​is also ​approved in China as a treatment for Type II diabetes.
  • The injection belongs to the class of GLP-1 receptor agonist drugs sold locally by drugmakers such as Novo Nordisk (NVO), ‌Eli Lilly (LLY), and ⁠Innovent Biologics (IVBIY).

data.title: AES Non-GAAP EPS of $0.81 beats by $0.20, revenue of $3.1B beats by $30M
data.description:
• AES press release (AES): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.81 beats by $0.20.
  • Revenue of $3.1B (+4.7% Y/Y) beats by $30M.

data.title: Rentokil Initial plc GAAP EPS of $0.11, revenue of $6.91B
data.description:
• Rentokil Initial plc press release (RTO): FY GAAP EPS of $0.11.
  • Revenue of $6.91B (+4.4% Y/Y).
  • FY25 Group Organic Revenue Growth of 2.6%, with improved performance in the second half.
  • Free Cash Flow1 of $615m, up 24.5% year-on-year, and Free Cash Flow Conversion of 98%, ahead of guidance due to real-estate sales and some one-off benefits within the overall working capital improvement.
  • Recommended final dividend of 8.24 cents, up 4.6%; total FY25 dividend of 12.39 cents, up 3%.
  • 2026 Outlook
     
    ●
     
    Despite some weather-related disruption in North America in January, and increased uncertainty from recent geopolitical events, we expect to deliver FY 2026 financial results in line with market expectations.

data.title: U.S. issues 30‑day waiver allowing India to buy Russian oil as Iran war disrupts supplies
data.description:
The United States on Thursday issued a 30-day waiver to India for purchasing crude from Moscow as the Iran war upends global supplies. The waiver on purchasing Russian oil is expected to help ease global supply worries, with India being the world’s fourth-biggest refiner and fifth-largest exporter of petroleum products. 

A license issued late on Thursday covers transactions related to Russian crude oil and petroleum products loaded onto vessels before March 5, so long as they're delivered to India and purchased by an Indian firm. The measure expires April 4 at 12:01 a.m. Washington time.

India faces risks from energy supply disruptions, with crude oil reserves sufficient for only about 25 days of demand. Around 40% of the country’s oil imports pass through the Strait of Hormuz from the Middle East.

Brent (CO1:COM) and WTI crude (CL1:COM) recouped some losses after falling over 1% on Friday and were last trading at $85.19 and $80.60 per barrel, respectively.

This “short-term measure will not provide significant financial benefit” to Russia as it only allows transactions of oil already stranded at sea, U.S. Secretary of the Treasury Scott Bessant said in a post on X.

The U.S. government, meanwhile, is taking steps to curb rising oil prices, including offering political risk insurance for tankers transiting the Gulf. U.S. crude prices have climbed about 20% this week on the back of the escalating conflict in the Middle East.

data.title: REalloys prices 2.7M share public offering at $18.50 each, stock dips
data.description:
• REalloys (ALOY) on Friday said that it has priced its previously announced underwritten public offering of 2,702,702 common shares at $18.50 per share.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about March 9, 2026.
  • The company's shares (ALOY) were down 10% in extended trading to $19.45; it has gained over 165% YTD.

data.title: Hunting PLC Non-GAAP EPS of $0.34, revenue of $1.02B misses by $70M
data.description:
• Hunting PLC press release (HNTIF): FY Non-GAAP EPS of $0.34.
  • Revenue of $1.02B (-2.9% Y/Y) misses by $70M.
  • Non-oil and gas revenue up 10%, to $82.9m.
  • Sales order book $358.0m comprising $120.7m of subsea and $98.6m of non-oil and gas opportunities.
  • Free cash flow of $96.6m – representing an EBITDA conversion of 71%.
  • Cash and bank / (borrowings) $62.9m, after c.$145m of net outflows related to acquisitions, share buybacks, treasury share purchases, and dividends.
  • 2026 EBITDA guidance of $145-$155m retained, with EBITDA to Free Cash Flow conversion targeted at 50% or greater.

data.title: Profound Medical outlines path to $85M revenue and profitable growth with TULSA expansion in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Profound Medical Corp. (PRN:CA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Arun Menawat, Chairman & CEO, emphasized the completion of recruitment for CAPTAIN, the first multicenter, randomized, controlled trial comparing a new technology to robotic radical prostatectomy, establishing "the foundational pillars of clinical evidence, validating TULSA as the new platform for prostate disease management." Menawat highlighted the imminent release of CAPTAIN’s primary safety and quality of life endpoints as a catalyst, stating, "CAPTAIN provides the first true apples-to-apples comparison of safety, quality of life and efficacy, the information required to support a new treatment paradigm."
  • Mathieu Burtnyk, President, announced that clinical outcomes from CAPTAIN will be presented ahead of schedule at the European Association of Urology meeting, including "complete 90-day perioperative results and the 6-month primary safety and quality of life endpoints." He noted, "Six-month quality of life outcomes are an increasingly important and modern endpoint."
  • Thomas Tamberrino, Chief Commercial Officer, reported that as of year-end, there were "78 TULSA-PRO sites" and a "TULSA-PRO qualified sales pipeline...at 110 new systems." He projected that, with current strategies, "we would be at $55 million in procedural revenue" and "around $85 million in annual revenue" when including service and capital sales, with profitability achieved at this scale.
  • Rashed Dewan, CFO, stated, "For the 3 months period ended, December 31, 2025, the company recorded revenue of $6 million, with $2.3 million from recurring revenue and $3.7 million from onetime sales of capital equipment."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated expectations to reach "high double-digit to low triple-digit revenue growth" in 2026. Tamberrino described, "Importantly, we believe we are now on a path to not just growth but profitable growth with this selling approach." Menawat stated, "We expect our cash burn to decline and eventually turn cash flow positive as our revenues continue to grow and our margin remains high."
  • No explicit numerical revenue or EPS guidance was provided for 2026, but the company reaffirmed its focus on expanding the installed base and driving utilization, aiming for "approximately 120" TULSA-PRO sites by year-end 2026.

Financial Results

  • Profound Medical recorded Q4 2025 revenue of $6 million, with recurring revenue at $2.3 million and capital equipment sales at $3.7 million. This represented a 43% increase from $4.2 million in Q4 2024.
  • Gross margin for Q4 2025 was 67%, down from 71% in Q4 2024, attributed to product mix and introductory pricing for new international distributors.
  • Total operating expenses for Q4 2025 were $11.4 million, compared to $11.3 million in the prior-year quarter.
  • The company reported a net loss of $8.2 million, or ($0.27) per share, versus a net loss of $4.9 million, or ($0.20) per share, in Q4 2024.
  • Cash as of December 31, 2025, stood at $59.7 million.

Q&A

  • Benjamin Haynor, Lake Street Capital Markets: Asked about commercial insurer reimbursement rates and success in overturning denials. Menawat responded, "The typical payments are between -- I would say, most of them are between 1.5x to 2.5x of Medicare...coverage and reversals from rejections were tracking better than 90% at this point."
  • Haynor: Inquired about the sequential decline in noncapital revenue and utilization. Menawat explained, "Every site is slowly but surely increasing usage...the pipeline is pretty strong. But over the long haul, I think that, we remain primarily a recurring revenue company."
  • John McAulay, Stifel: Sought clarity on recurring revenue per procedure and future revenue mix. Menawat clarified, "Our price for disposables is $5,500 fixed. There is absolutely no discounting on the disposable price."
  • Michael Freeman, Raymond James: Asked about early release of CAPTAIN data and impact on reimbursement. Menawat commented, "We are very careful...our trials are pristine...I do expect that the earlier data will suddenly gives us more confidence in getting coverage decisions this year."
  • Scott McAuley, Paradigm Capital: Asked about BPH module adoption and international market progress. Menawat stated, "At least 10 sites...have already started using it. By midyear, we will have at least 30, 40 sites using it."
  • Christopher Potter, Northern Border Advisors: Queried about procedure utilization per site. Menawat responded, "We think 50 is a very reasonable number...We have sites today that are doing well over 100."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on reimbursement, revenue mix, and site utilization, with a tone that was inquisitive to slightly skeptical, evidenced by detailed queries on recurring revenue fluctuations, per-procedure economics, and near-term utilization growth.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, especially in prepared remarks. During Q&A, management remained composed and provided detailed clarifications, using phrases such as "we are very, very satisfied with the numbers that we're seeing" and "we are comfortable and confident" regarding growth and cash flow prospects.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence level remained high, with a greater emphasis on strategic execution and operational improvements, while analyst tone remained focused on accountability for financial targets and utilization ramp.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue rose from $5.3 million in Q3 2025 to $6 million in Q4 2025, but gross margin declined from 74.3% to 67% due to introductory pricing for new international distributors.
  • The net loss widened to $8.2 million from $8 million, while cash increased significantly to $59.7 million from $24.8 million in the previous quarter.
  • Installed TULSA-PRO sites grew from 70 to 78, and the qualified sales pipeline increased from 93 to 110 new systems.
  • Management's tone shifted from emphasizing product launches and early BPH module feedback in Q3 to focusing on clinical evidence from CAPTAIN and international expansion in Q4.
  • Analysts continued to scrutinize revenue composition, utilization growth, and reimbursement, consistent with prior quarters, but with more granular questions on revenue per procedure and capital versus recurring revenue mix.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited challenges with gross margin pressure due to new market introductory pricing and product mix for international distributors.
  • There are operational scaling risks as the company navigates "increasing our logistics and operations side," including plans for a U.S. warehouse and expanded ERP systems.
  • The company faces the challenge of driving higher utilization per installed site to reach profitability targets.
  • Analyst questions reflected concerns about recurring revenue volatility, site ramp-up, and international expansion pacing.

Final Takeaway

Profound Medical management communicated a clear path toward profitable growth, underpinned by increasing TULSA-PRO installations, expanding international presence, and anticipated clinical milestones from the CAPTAIN trial. The company’s focus on high-margin recurring revenue, operational scaling, and reimbursement advances positions it to target $85 million in annual revenue and profitability, while ongoing clinical data releases and strategic partnerships are expected to drive further adoption and market penetration in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Omada Health outlines 22% revenue growth target for 2026 while launching new GLP-1 and cholesterol programs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Omada Health, Inc. (OMDA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Sean Duffy described 2025 as a “milestone year,” highlighting the company’s public debut, 53% annual revenue growth, and first quarter of GAAP profitability. Duffy reported, “We became a public company, delivered 53% revenue growth for the year and achieved GAAP profitability for the first time in Q4.” He emphasized Omada’s scale, technology, and market tailwinds, noting 886,000 total members at year-end, up 55% from 2024, and expanded gross margins. Duffy also pointed to Omada’s leadership in GLP-1 companion care, stating, “Employer demand to maximize the value of their GLP-1 investment and reduced waste drove significant adoption of our GLP-1 Care Track.” He further highlighted the launch of GLP-1 prescribing and a new cholesterol program, and the broader reach of the multi-condition care platform.
  • President Wei-Li Shao credited multi-condition adoption and GLP-1 demand for membership and revenue growth, reporting, “We ended the year with 886,000 members, up 55% year-over-year...we added 314,000 net new members, compared to 182,000 in 2024.” Shao noted Omada now serves over 25 million estimated eligible lives and referenced the initial commercial launch of the cholesterol program with a large enterprise customer.
  • CFO Steven Cook stated, “Revenue in Q4 was $76 million, up 58% year-over-year. For the full year, revenue was $260 million, up 53% compared to 2024.” Cook noted margin expansion and positive adjusted EBITDA, adding, “Q4 adjusted gross margin reached 73% in Q4, an all-time high...we reached positive adjusted EBITDA a full year ahead of expectations.”

Outlook

  • Omada projects 2026 revenue in the range of $312 million to $322 million, with the midpoint reflecting 22% growth over 2025. Adjusted EBITDA is forecasted between $7 million and $15 million, with the midpoint $5 million above last year. CFO Cook stated, “We expect 2026 revenue in the range of $312 million to $322 million, with the midpoint reflecting 22% growth over 2025.” Cook emphasized guidance is based on the current member base and does not include significant contributions from new programs such as GLP-1 prescribing, Flex Care, or the cholesterol program.
  • Management highlighted that guidance assumes no major improvement in enrollment conversion or member engagement, and upside may be possible if new initiatives gain traction in 2026.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue was $76 million and full year revenue was $260 million, driven by GLP-1 program adoption, multi-condition expansion, and improved enrollment campaigns. GAAP gross margin reached 71% in Q4 and 66% for the full year. Adjusted gross margin was 73% in Q4 and 68% for the year.
  • Q4 GAAP net income was $5 million, compared to a net loss of $8 million in Q4 2024. Full year GAAP net loss narrowed to $13 million from $47 million in 2024. Q4 adjusted EBITDA was $8 million, while full year adjusted EBITDA was $6 million, both showing marked improvement over the prior year.
  • Omada ended 2025 with $222 million in cash and no debt, having repaid its $30 million credit facility. Cook noted the company generated positive operating cash flow for the full year.

Q&A

  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: Asked if 2026 guidance is based only on the existing business and if base business growth is decelerating. Cook responded that guidance is built “off of 886,000 exit members,” assumes “no material improvement across those metrics throughout the course of the year,” and does not include upside from new product launches.
  • Sean Dodge, BMO: Asked about the economics and strategy behind GLP-1 Flex Care. Duffy explained, “Employers pay Omada and would pay Omada more for the GLP-1 Flex Care offering because that includes clinical evaluation, prescribing lab ordering and Omada lifestyle and behavioral support.”
  • Craig Hettenbach, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about Omada’s insulation from AI disruption. Duffy emphasized, “The value of unique data sets...allows us to customize and personalize care in a way that's unique and in a way that's valuable.”
  • Ryan MacDonald, Needham: Sought clarity on ARPU trends and program mix. Cook explained ARPU is expected to stay “relatively flat” and new product categories could drive incremental ARPU as adoption grows.
  • Elizabeth Anderson, Evercore: Asked about gross margin improvement and the $2 million Q4 one-time adjustment. Cook clarified, “We did have a $2 million onetime true-up...We don't expect that to recur on a go-forward basis.”
  • Stanislav Berenshteyn, Wells Fargo: Asked about retention and the impact of OmadaSpark and Meal Map. Shao said early results show “members who interact with a lot of Spark...demonstrating higher levels of ongoing engagement.”
  • Richard Close, Canaccord: Inquired about the impact of GLP-1 price declines. Duffy stated, “We believe these lower price points actually have the potential to increase GLP-1 utilization, which increases access to the medicines and thus, increases the need for Care Track services like Omada.”
  • Carly Buecker, Barclays: Asked about adoption curves for new programs. Duffy and Shao said the company expects to accelerate adoption timelines for new launches compared to historical experience.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth sustainability, new product economics, margin trends, and guidance assumptions, with a neutral to slightly positive tone. Questions were largely clarifying rather than confrontational, with requests for detail on revenue visibility, ARPU, and new program impact.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic stance in prepared remarks, using phrases like “we are entering 2026 with momentum” and “we are very pleased with our 2025 results.” During Q&A, management was measured and transparent, especially when discussing guidance assumptions and the limited inclusion of new programs in baseline forecasts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone showed increased confidence, particularly following the achievement of profitability and ahead-of-schedule adjusted EBITDA. Analyst tone remained focused on details of growth and profitability but was less skeptical than in prior quarters.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism to a more confident stance, reflecting outperformance in 2025 and a clear 22% revenue growth target for 2026. The company’s strategic focus expanded to emphasize rapid GLP-1 adoption, new prescribing and cholesterol programs, and AI-powered engagement tools, compared to last quarter’s announcement of GLP-1 prescribing as a planned initiative.
  • Key metrics improved: Q4 revenue rose to $76 million from $68 million in Q3; membership increased to 886,000 from 831,000; and the company posted its first GAAP net income in Q4. Gross and adjusted margins reached new highs. Management’s confidence was notably higher, supported by operational milestones.
  • Analyst questions shifted from the mechanics of new initiatives to their potential impact and scalability, with continued attention to revenue mix and margin sustainability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged that 2026 guidance does not include significant revenue from new programs such as GLP-1 prescribing, Flex Care, and cholesterol, representing both a risk and a source of potential upside.
  • The company noted government policy changes and evolving GLP-1 pricing as areas to monitor, with Duffy saying, “We'll have to see how this evolves.”
  • Seasonality and a $2 million one-time Q4 adjustment may affect quarter-over-quarter comparisons in 2026, as flagged by Cook.

Final Takeaway

Omada Health closed 2025 with rapid membership expansion, significant revenue growth, and its first profitable quarter, all while launching new GLP-1 and cholesterol programs and embedding AI across its platform. Management projects 22% revenue growth for 2026, with guidance based on existing business and upside potential from new initiatives, underpinned by a strong balance sheet and continued operating leverage.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cooper Companies raises EPS and free cash flow guidance for 2026 amid robust MyDay and MiSight momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Cooper Companies (COO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Albert White reported "a strong start to the fiscal year, highlighted by product launches, outstanding profitability and robust cash flow." White emphasized progress on three strategic priorities: consistent market share gains for CooperVision, operational excellence translating to strong earnings and free cash flow, and disciplined capital allocation including $92 million in stock repurchases and debt reduction. He highlighted the global rollout of the MyDay daily silicone hydrogel portfolio and robust adoption of MiSight, particularly in EMEA and Japan.
  • White acknowledged regional dynamics: "The Americas and EMEA strengthened and have excellent commercial momentum. Japan weighed on our Asia Pac results, but we're executing on product launches and investing to restore growth in the region."
  • White stated, "Q1 earnings exceeded the top end of our guidance range, and those earnings translated into a healthy $159 million in free cash flow. Given our strong start to the year, we're raising guidance for both earnings and free cash flow."
  • On the strategic review announced in December, White said, "the review is progressing as planned with active engagement from our Board and advisers. We will communicate outcomes if we have something definitive to share or when the process is complete."
  • CFO Brian Andrews said, "Gross margin was 68.1%, exceeding expectations driven primarily by a lighter mix of low-margin Asia Pac revenue at CooperVision... Operating expenses rose only modestly and improved as a percentage of sales, declining from 43.6% to 41.2% year-over-year, reflecting the benefits of the reorganization executed in fiscal Q4 of last year."

Outlook

  • Andrews provided guidance for fiscal 2026: "consolidated revenues of roughly $4.3 billion to $4.35 billion, reflecting organic growth of roughly 4.5% to 5.5%. CooperVision revenue is expected to be in the range of $2.9 billion to $2.93 billion, up 4.5% to 5.5% organically. And CooperSurgical is expected to be in the range of $1.4 billion to $1.41 billion, up 4% to 5% organically."
  • Non-GAAP EPS guidance was raised to $4.58 to $4.66 "reflecting our Q1 beat and stronger expected operational performance."
  • Free cash flow guidance increased to $600 million to $625 million for 2026. For 2026 through 2028, the target remains "to generate more than $2.2 billion of free cash flow."
  • Guidance on tariffs, interest, and tax rates remain unchanged.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenues were $1.024 billion, up 6.2% or up 2.9% organically for Q1.
  • CooperVision revenue was $695 million, up 7.6% or up 3.3% organically. CooperSurgical revenue was $329 million, up 3.3% or up 2.2% organically.
  • Non-GAAP earnings grew 20% to $1.10. Free cash flow reached $159 million. The company repurchased 1.1 million shares for $92 million, made a final $50 million payment related to the Cook acquisition, and reduced net debt to $2.4 billion.
  • Operating margin improved to 26.9%, with operating income up 13.9%. Gross margin reached 68.1%.

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Johnson, Robert W. Baird & Co.: Asked about reconciling the 10% calendar Q4 CVI growth versus recent quarters. White explained, "It's literally just a matter of months and shipment of product."
  • Johnson inquired about regaining above-market growth. White pointed to strong momentum in the Americas and EMEA, but noted Asia Pac, especially Japan, remains a challenge due to softness in older hydrogel products. He indicated, "Asia Pac will probably be down because of it. But then all of that success...happens in Q3, and you're going to have Asia Pac growing again."
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Probed about Middle East exposure. White responded, "the Middle East is about 2% of our sales...it could impact fertility because there's a decent amount of fertility business."
  • Biegelsen asked about margin phasing and tariffs. Andrews stated, "We've assumed $24 million in the year...a 10% tariff makes very little impact."
  • Jason Bednar, Piper Sandler: Inquired about new fit activity and industry price dynamics. White said, "we are taking wearers in both of those as we did this past quarter" and expressed confidence in positive pricing, though cautioned about Asia Pac competition.
  • Jonathan Block, Stifel: Asked about CVI organic growth underperformance and legal-related add backs. White attributed it to Japan's legacy products, while Andrews cited "$6.7 million was related to other legal-related matters."
  • Young Li, Jefferies: Sought updates on supply dynamics and Paragard. White confirmed supply constraints are resolved and contract wins are ongoing; Paragard was down 7% for the quarter.
  • Other analysts explored private label growth, MiSight market opportunity in Japan, the impact of Stellest, and R&D investments in myopia control.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism but pressed on Asia Pac headwinds, CVI growth, and competitive threats, often probing for more detail on regional outlook and supply dynamics. Tone was neutral with pockets of skepticism on Japan and fertility.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone, repeatedly emphasizing execution, operational excellence, and visibility into growth drivers. Examples include White's "we are confident the region will return to growth in fiscal Q3" and Andrews' "I'm proud of the operational excellence we're seeing across the organization."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's sentiment shifted from cautious optimism to increased confidence, particularly around improved execution, cost controls, and the ability to deliver on raised guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for EPS and free cash flow was raised following a Q1 beat, compared to maintained guidance last quarter.
  • The strategic review remains ongoing, with no new details. Management continues to emphasize operational leverage and AI-driven efficiencies.
  • Analysts' focus on Asia Pac weakness, supply chain recovery, and competitive dynamics persisted, while management shifted to a more assertive outlook on margin improvement and regional recovery.
  • Revenue growth in CooperVision and CooperSurgical was slightly lower than in the previous quarter, and growth in Japan was again a notable drag.
  • Management confidence in overcoming Asia Pac challenges and driving consistent growth in Q3 and Q4 is more prominent this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued softness in Japan and legacy hydrogel products as a drag on Asia Pac, with recovery not expected until Q3.
  • Uncertainty in the Middle East, which represents 2% of sales, could impact fertility business.
  • Competitive pressures in Asia Pac and on Paragard were acknowledged, but no major updates on new entrants. White said, "We have not caved on price or anything along those lines."
  • Legal-related expenses were higher this quarter.
  • Management reiterated active engagement in the strategic review process but provided no timeline for completion.

Final Takeaway

The Cooper Companies entered fiscal 2026 with strong momentum, driven by robust performance in premium MyDay and MiSight portfolios, operational efficiencies, and disciplined capital allocation. Raised earnings and free cash flow guidance reflect confidence in the company’s ability to deliver sustained growth and improved profitability, supported by continued investment in innovation and expansion across key markets. Management remains focused on overcoming regional challenges, especially in Asia Pac, and executing on its strategic priorities to maximize long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump says U.S. must have a role in choosing Iran's next leader - Reuters
